coredata.dc:title: An all-in-one smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent device for visual and quantitative detection of glutathione
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Yuan
Bai, Bingfeng
Mao, Zenghui
Yang, Xiao
Shi, Yi
Zhang, Bo
Quan, Ke
Qing, Zhihe
coredata.dc:description: The daily consumption of foods abundant in Glutathione (GSH) can be supplemented to maintain the homeostasis of GSH in human health and alleviate pathologies resulting from abnormal GSH levels. The fluorescence-based visual determination of GSH has gradually attracted the attention of researchers due to its robust performance and versatile implementation. However, the current GSH visual strategy primarily relies on variations in fluorescence intensity at a single emission wavelength, which poses challenges for naked-eye and portable readout, as well as distorted signals caused by complex matrix effects in real samples. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on carbon dots (CDs) combined with an all-in-one 3D-printed smartphone-based device was successfully developed for low-cost, visual and rapid detection of GSH without the need for an external excitation light source. The ratiometric fluorescent materials were synthesized by conjugating blue carbon dots (B-CDs) with yellow carbon dots (Y-CDs) through the utilization of selected Cu2+ ions. The resulting mechanism demonstrated that the coordination interaction between Cu2+ and residual aromatic amino groups in Y-CDs (Y-CDs-Cu2+) contributed to a newly emitted peak at 580 nm, thereby inducing fluorescence resonance energy transfer from B-CDs to Y-CDs-Cu2+. A linear correlation was found between GSH concentrations and R/B values in the range of 10–100 μM, with a limit of detection observed at 4.8 μM. By utilizing this portable device in combination with RGB analysis, the quantitative detection of GSH can be achieved even in complex food matrices such as tomatoes and grapes. The universality of this all-in-one device was further validated by pre-spraying CDs onto a paper strip for visual measurement of GSH. This work offers a portable, visual, and accessible approach to evaluating food safety and nutrition, thereby demonstrating significant economic value and holding profound implications for human health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bioanalysis
Ratiometric fluorescence
Portable detection
Carbon dots
Glutathione

Determination of GSH in real food samples
Preparation and characterization of ratiometric sensor
The mechanism exploration of Cu2+-linked ratiometric sensor
The response behavior of GSH to Cu2+-BY-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Schematic diagram of CD-based ratiometric sensor combined with a 3D-printed smartphone-based device for the visual and rapid determination of GSH.
The TEM image of B-CDs (A) and Y-CDs (B). The UV–vis absorption spectra of B-CDs (C) and Y-CDs (D). The fluorescence excitation spectra of B-CDs (E) and Y-CDs (F) at different excitation wavelength.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of BY-CDs to 10 μM Cu2+ and twelve other analytes. (B) Cu2p spectra before and after adding Cu2+−to BY-CDs. (C–D) The Fluorescence intensity changes of B-CDs and Y-CDs in the presence of Cu2+. (E) The UV–vis absorption spectra of Y-CDs-Cu2+, and the emission spectra of B-CDs. (F) Fluorescence decay plot of B-CDs before and after adding Cu2+.

(A) The fluorescence intensity of BY-CDs in the presence of different analyte. and (B) The fluorescence intensity changes of Cu2+-BY-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of GSH. (C) Relationship between GSH concentration and fluorescence intensity ratio F580/F475, Inset: Linear response of F580/F475. (D) The ratio value of F580/F475 in the presence of GSH and eight other analytes responding to Cu2+- BY-CDs.

(A) The illustration of 3D-printed smartphone-based device mode. (B) CIE chromaticity diagram of Cu2+-BY-CDs in various concentrations of GSH solution. (C) Color change of Cu2+-BY-CDs and corresponding RGB values upon 0–200 μM GSH. (D) Linear relationship between the concentrations of GSH and R/B. (E–F) The color changes of different sensing systems on paper strip under sunlight and ultraviolet light.
Comparison of determination results between this method and commercial kit detection.

head.ce:title: An all-in-one smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent device for visual and quantitative detection of glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Cytochemistry, School of Food and Bioengineering, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha, 410114, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Cytochemistry
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Changsha University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Cytochemistry, School of Food and Bioengineering, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha 410114, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Regional Hereditary Birth Defects Prevention and Control, Changsha Hospital for Maternal and Child Health Care Affiliated to Hunan Normal University, Changsha, 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Regional Hereditary Birth Defects Prevention and Control
Changsha Hospital for Maternal and Child Health Care Affiliated to Hunan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Regional Hereditary Birth Defects Prevention and Control, Changsha Hospital for Maternal and Child Health Care Affiliated to Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410083 (China)

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The daily consumption of foods abundant in Glutathione (GSH) can be supplemented to maintain the homeostasis of GSH in human health and alleviate pathologies resulting from abnormal GSH levels. The fluorescence-based visual determination of GSH has gradually attracted the attention of researchers due to its robust performance and versatile implementation. However, the current GSH visual strategy primarily relies on variations in fluorescence intensity at a single emission wavelength, which poses challenges for naked-eye and portable readout, as well as distorted signals caused by complex matrix effects in real samples. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on carbon dots (CDs) combined with an all-in-one 3D-printed smartphone-based device was successfully developed for low-cost, visual and rapid detection of GSH without the need for an external excitation light source. The ratiometric fluorescent materials were synthesized by conjugating blue carbon dots (B-CDs) with yellow carbon dots (Y-CDs) through the utilization of selected Cu2+ ions. The resulting mechanism demonstrated that the coordination interaction between Cu2+ and residual aromatic amino groups in Y-CDs (Y-CDs-Cu2+) contributed to a newly emitted peak at 580 nm, thereby inducing fluorescence resonance energy transfer from B-CDs to Y-CDs-Cu2+. A linear correlation was found between GSH concentrations and R/B values in the range of 10–100 μM, with a limit of detection observed at 4.8 μM. By utilizing this portable device in combination with RGB analysis, the quantitative detection of GSH can be achieved even in complex food matrices such as tomatoes and grapes. The universality of this all-in-one device was further validated by pre-spraying CDs onto a paper strip for visual measurement of GSH. This work offers a portable, visual, and accessible approach to evaluating food safety and nutrition, thereby demonstrating significant economic value and holding profound implications for human health.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH), as the essential endogenous biothiol, plays a pivotal role in maintaining cellular redox homeostasis, metabolism, and signal transduction [1]. Abnormal GSH levels is closely associated with the pathogenesis such as Parkinson's disease, cystic fibrosis and immune disorders [2,3]. Alternatively, the consistent consumption of foods rich in GSH from diverse dietary sources compensates for maintaining GSH balance and alleviate adverse health conditions [4]. Moreover, owing to its exceptional antioxidant properties, GSH has been incorporated in foods to suppress enzymatic and non-enzymatic browning or improve flavor profiles [5]. Therefore, quantifying and comparing the levels of GSH in different food sources can provide a useful reference for daily GSH intake and valuable guidance for producing functional foods enriched with GSH [6,7]. Considerable endeavors have been devoted to the advancement of sensing techniques for the determination of GSH, including mass spectrometry (MS), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), visual sensor [8–11]. The visual detection typically involves direct observation with the naked eye, utilizing human photoreceptors in the retina to perceive light within the wavelength ranged from 400 nm to 800 nm. It offers the advantages of portability and cost-effectiveness, with the representative mode being colorimetric transductions [12,13]. With the increasing demand for more reliable and sensitive results, the fluorescence-based visual sensing has gradually attracted researchers' attention due to its favorable performance and versatile implementation. Among this, fluorescent nanomaterials such as inorganic nanoparticles, organic small molecules, lanthanide complexes have been developed for visualization detection of GSH [14,15]. However, the aforementioned analytical strategy primarily relies on changes in fluorescence intensity at a single emission wavelength, posing challenges for naked-eye readout due to potential limitations in human vision sensitivity towards subtle variations in fluorescence brightness [16]. Additionally, the accuracy of single-signal mode is susceptible to external fluctuations that are independent of the target [17–19], such as intricate matrix effects in food accompanied by distorted fluorescence signals. Additionally, instrumental aspects regarding the excitation source and environmental factors including temperature and humidity also contribute to this impairment. Therefore, the satisfaction of a visualization sensor for GSH necessitates not only minimal cost and ease of operation, but also exceptional accuracy in complex matrix.

The ratiometric fluorescence system emerges as the most promising candidate to compensate for these limitations by offering quantitative analysis through measuring the intensity of multiple fluorescence emission peaks that exhibit variations in response to the analyte [20]. This inherent self-calibration effect significantly mitigates interference within the sensor. Furthermore, universal ratiometry possesses the capability to continuously adjust tonality based on the output of different color [21], thereby facilitating improved visual identification by the naked eyes compared to relying solely on fluorescence brightness [22,23]. Additionally, the hue undergoes variations along with changes in the intensity of the three primary colors (red-green-blue, RGB), thus enabling the utilization of signal ratio values between the three primary colors, such as G/R, for quantifying target concentrations using portable terminal devices like smartphones. By integrating high-definition cameras and advanced data analysis ability, smartphones allow for the conversion of captured fluorescent images into quantifiable RGB values [24], thereby significantly improving signal discrimination through comprehensive colour spectrum assessment than naked-eyes readout. This advancement has greatly promoted the miniaturization of fluorescence sensors and bolstered their application for portable on-site detection.

Carbon dots (CDs), as promising luminescent nanomaterials, have attracted enormous attention due to their low cost, strong photobleaching resistance and abundant surface functional groups including carboxyl (-COOH), hydroxyl (-OH), and amino groups (-NH2) [25]. As a result, they can be widely applied in the superior detection of residues of pesticides drugs and veterinary, pathogenic microorganism, food additives [26]. More significantly, the emission wavelength of CDs can be finely tuned across the visible to the near-infrared regions of the electromagnetic spectrum owing to their size-dependent and composition-dependent characteristics. Herein, we propose a CD-based ratiometric fuorescence strategy integrated with a 3D-printed smartphone-based device for the visual and rapid determination of GSH in foods (Fig. 1 ), without requiring an external excitation light source. The ratiometric fluorescent materials were obtained by linking yellow carbon dots (Y-CDs) and blue carbon dots (B-CDs) through the use of selected Cu2+ (Cu2+-BY-CDs). The resulting mechanism demonstrated that the coordination interaction between Cu2+ and residual aromatic NH2 groups (Y-CDs-Cu2+) in Y-CDs contributed to a newly emitted peak at 580 nm in Y-CDs, thereby inducing fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from B-CDs to Y-CDs-Cu2+ due to their overlapping spectra. When GSH was introduced, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs demonstrated a gradual increase while the fluorescence of Y-CDs decreased due to the chelation interaction between Cu2+ and GSH. Lastly, we adapted these ratiometric fluorescent materials to a 3D-printed device that integrated with a 365 nm UV light source, thus enabling GSH detection using a smartphone-based RGB analysis in foods such as tomatoes and grapes. The universality of this portable device can be further extended to pre-spray CDs onto a paper strip for visual measurement of GSH. Overall, the well-established ratiometric Cu2+-BY-CDs system not only possesses a visual and fast response to GSH but also exhibits portability, achieving practical application in real food samples.

Citric acid and ethylenediamine were purchased from Shanghai Sangon BioEngineering Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China), O-phenylene diamine was provided by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China), CuSO4·5H2O, various metal salts, and amino acids were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China), and glutathione acquired was from Sa'en Chemical Technology Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). GSH detection kit was purchased from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute (Nanjing, China). All reagents used in the experiments were analytically pure and can be used directly without additional treatment unless otherwise stated. Ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ•cm) was produced using a Millipore water purification system.

The carbon dots’ morphology was examined using a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). Fluorescence spectra were investigated by a PTI QM40 transient/steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured with a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Infrared spectra of carbon dots were obtained using a YQ105-6700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (NICOLET, USA). Fluorescence lifetimes were determined using a transient/steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Zeta potentials were recorded on a NanoZS90 nano-particle Size potential analyzer (Malvern, UK). Y-CDs was synthesized with P70F20L-DG microwave oven (Glanz, China). The smartphone (HONOR70) was purchased from China Honor Terminal Co., LTD.

Synthesis of B-CDs: The synthesis of B-CDs was achieved through a one-pot hydrothermal method. A mixture of 3.0 g of citric acid and ethylenediamine (C2H8N2) in 30 mL of ultrapure water was thoroughly blended and then transferred to a Teflon-lined 50 mL autoclave before being heated at 150 °C for 5 h. The solution was cooled to ambient temperature and filtered using a 0.22 μm polyether sulfone membrane, followed by dialysis against ultrapure water (MV = 3500 Da) for 48 h to obtain the B-CDs stock solution, which was refrigerated at 4 °C until use.

Synthesis of Y-CDs: The Y-CDs were synthesized using a previously reported microwave-assisted method. A solution of 0.4 g of O-phenylene diamine (OPD) was subjected to microwave irradiation at a power of 700W for 20 min. After cooled to room temperature, the reaction product was dispersed again in aqueous solution to prepare the initial Y-CDs. The Y-CDs solution underwent filtration through a 0.22 μm PES membrane and subsequent dialysis against ultrapure water (MW = 3000 Da) for 24 h. The final stock solution of Y-CDs was stored at 4 °C until use.

The fluorescence sensor for GSH detection was prepared by combining 790 μL of B-CDs solution (0.02 mg/mL), 190 μL of Y-CDs solution (7.35 mg/mL), and 10 μL of 10 μM Cu2+ solution. Various concentrations of GSH (0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 70, 100, 150, 200 μM) were added to the sensor solution. After incubation at 25 °C for 18 min, the fluorescence spectra of the sensor were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 375 nm for GSH analysis. The selectivity of GSH detection was evaluated by testing interfering substances including sucrose, glucose, glycine, glutamate, alanine, arginine, and cysteine. All substances were tested at a concentration of 150 μM under identical experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GSH determination combined with a smartphone-assisted device
ce:section.ce:para: The all-in-one sensor device is fabricated by integrating a 3D-printed model with a smartphone. The sensor model contains a battery pack, sample tank, filter, and a 365 nm UV LED light source. Various concentrations of GSH were typically introduced into the sensing solution for analysis. Subsequently, the test tube containing the GSH samples was positioned within the dedicated sample cell of the sensor device, and fluorescence images are captured using the smartphone's camera module. To extract quantitative information regarding GSH levels, the RGB analysis software “color picker” was employed to analyze acquired fluorescence photographs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GSH in real food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The suitability of the fluorescent sensor was evaluated by detecting GSH in actual food samples, including tomatoes, cucumbers and grapes, obtained from two local supermarkets in Changsha, China. The food samples were naturally washed and dried before being accurately weighed, crushed, and homogenized using a commercial mixer. Subsequently, the resulting samples were sonicated for 5 min in an ultrasonic cell crusher followed by 30 min in an ultrasonic cleaning machine to ensure complete extraction of GSH. After being subjected to centrifugation for 15 min (5000 rpm, 4 °C), the supernatant was separated by employing a PES filter with a pore size of 0.45 μm for the purpose of eliminating impurities. Then 10 μL as-prepared food samples or different concentrations of GSH (5 μM, 10 μM, and 20 μM) were introduced to Cu2+-BY-CDs system and incubated at 25 °C for reaction. The data were collected using the POCT device. The kit detection assay involves the reaction of GSH with DTNB to produce a yellow compound, which can be quantitatively determined through absorbance value at 405 nm. Mean values were calculated from three independent experiments, and standard deviations were also determined.

Paper-based sensors were utilized for on-site GSH biosensing in conjunction with a smartphone. Initially, Whatman No. 1 filter paper was punched into circular test papers using a hole puncher. Subsequently, each strip was immersed multiple times in a mixed solution comprising B-CDs (790 μL, 0.02 mg/mL) and Y-CDs (190 μL, 7.35 mg/mL), followed by oven-drying at 40 °C for 60 min. Then 10 μL of Cu2+ standard solution (10 μM) and varying concentrations of GSH solution (0, 10, 20, 50, 70, 100, 150 μM) were then added dropwise. After an incubation period of 18 min, the fluorescence color change of the test paper under UV light was captured by a smartphone within the sensor platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of ratiometric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Blue CDs and Yellow CDs are two core elements in our Cu2+-BY-CDs system, which were prepared using hydrothermal and microwave-assisted method, respectively. The morphology of B-CDs and Y-CDs was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as depicted in Fig. 2 A and B. Both CDs exhibited well-dispersed spherical shapes with a particle size distribution of about 5 nm. In the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra (Fig. 2C and D, Fig. S1), B-CDs displayed distinct absorption peaks at 235 nm and 350 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of CC bonds and n-π* transition of C–N bonds, respectively. The B-CDs solution appeared colorless under sunlight but exhibited a vibrant blue fluorescence upon exposure to a 365 nm ultraviolet light source. The Y-CDs exhibited characteristic absorption peaks at 230 nm and 290 nm, resulting in a pale-yellow appearance under sunlight that transformed into bright yellow fluorescence upon exposure to a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. As displayed in Fig. 2E, the B-CDs appeared an emission peak at approximately 450 nm upon excitation at 365 nm. In addition, Y-CDs exhibited an excitation-dependent behavior with obvious intensity changes of fluorescence emission peaks when excited within the range of 200–400 nm (Fig. 2F). The quantum yield (QY) of both CDs can be determined by comparing them with quinine sulfate, which was used as a reference compound dissolved in a solution of 0.1 M H2SO4, with the QY at 360 nm determined to be 0.54 [27]. To investigate the composition of surface functional groups on both CDs, the FTIR analysis was conducted as illustrated in Figs. S2–S3. The B-CDs showed a prominently broad absorption peak in the range of 3000–3700 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibration of the N–H bond. The observed absorption at 2909 cm−1, 1638 cm−1, 1385 cm−1, and 1135 cm−1 corresponded respectively to the stretching vibrations of the C–H, CO, C–N, and C–O bond. The presence of –NH2 on the surfaces of Y-CDs can be evidenced by the distinct broad band observed within the range of 3000–3700 cm−1. The peaks at 1638 cm−1 and 1514 cm−1 were associated with the CN and N–H stretching vibration. Moreover, the peaks at 1370 cm−1 and 1139 cm−1 accounted for the aromatic CC and C–N stretching frequencies [28]. The absorption spectrum displayed by the BY-CDs was similar to that of individual CDs. (Fig. S4). In addition, the as-prepared B-CDs and Y-CDs exhibited excellent stability during one week of storage with minimal changes in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S5).

Motivated by the ability of metal ions to form covalent bond with CDs and their strong coordination interactions with GSH, a screening of metal ions was then conducted to specifically respond to the hybrid system comprising Y-CDs and B-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, it was evident that the majority of metal ions exhibited negligible response to the mixture solution of B-CDs and Y-CDs (BY-CDs). However, Fe3+, Fe2+, and Co2+ displayed slight interaction, while Cu2+ demonstrated a significant response to the nanohybrid solution. It has been proven that the zeta potential of Y-CDs and B-CDs solutions showcased a decrease in negative charge from −11.4 to- 4.3 mV, and from −11.9 to +5.08 mV (Fig. S6) when Cu2+ was added, indicating the presence of interactions between both CDs and Cu2+. Through XPS investigation of B-CDs and Y-CDs after the addition of Cu2+, as shown in Fig. S7, the peaks at 283 eV, 402 eV, 535 eV, 932 eV belong to characteristic peaks of C1s, N1s, O1s and Cu2p respectively, also indicating an interaction between Cu2+ and hybrid BY-CDs. Furthermore, in the high-resolution Cu2p spectrum (Fig. 3B), peaks corresponding to Cu2p3/2 at 932.48 eV and Cu2p1/2 at 951.8 eV were observed, further confirming the involvement of Cu2+ in the coordination reaction with CDs. Upon investigating the response of different concentrations of Cu2+ to hybrid BY-CDs, the fluorescence intensity at 475 nm exhibited a noticeable decrease, while at 580 nm it showed a significant increase, as the concentration of Cu2+ varied from 0 μM to 50 μM upon excitation at 375 nm, resulting in a change in solution color from blue to yellow (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism exploration of Cu2+-linked ratiometric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing mechanism of Cu2+ to B-CDs and Y-CDs was analyzed subsequently. As shown in Fig. 3C, the addition of Cu2+ to B-CDs resulted in negligible alteration in the fluorescence intensity at 475 nm, suggesting that Cu2+ had minimal impact on the emission wavelength of B-CDs. In contrast, as illustrated in Fig. 3D, a significant enhancement effect on the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs at 580 nm was observed when Cu2+ was introduced into Y-CDs, accompanied by a conspicuous change in solution color into bright yellow. We then investigated the fluorescence enhancement mechanism of Cu2+ on Y-CDs. As depicted in Fig. S9, a distinct absorption peak at 420 nm in the UV–vis spectrum was emerged after the addition of Cu2+ to Y-CDs. The absorption intensity elevated with the concentration of Cu2+, ranging from 0 μM to 40 μM, and is accompanied by color transition from pale yellow to bright yellow. Integrating FTIR analysis obtained from Y-CDs, we speculated that the formation of a complex between aromatic amino on the surface of Y-CDs due to incomplete carbonization of the precursor: o-phenylenediamine (OPD) during synthesis and Cu2+, may account for the newly formed absorption peak [29]. Subsequently, this speculation was validated by quantifying OPD's UV absorption spectra at various concentrations of Cu2+ (Fig. S9). The presence of a similar absorption peak at 418 nm was ascribed to the selective oxidation of OPD by Cu2+, as indicated in previous studies [30,31]. Therefore, it was concluded that the lone pair electrons present on the aromatic nitrogen within OPD can quench the fluorescence emitted by Y-CDs through photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [28,32]. However, in the presence of Cu2+, a complex can be formed with aromatic –NH2 on the surface of carbon dots through shared electron pairs, thereby enhancing fluorescence by inhibiting PET occurrence [33].

In order to elucidate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of B-CDs in the hybrid BY-CDs system after adding Cu2+, further investigation was conducted on its spectrum properties as shown in Fig. 3E. The fluorescence emission peak of B-CDs largely overlapped with the UV absorption peak of the Y-CDs-Cu2+complex, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs may be caused by either internal rate effect (IFE) or FRET [28]. IFE is a static quenching process, while FRET belongs to a dynamic quenching process, which can be distinguished by alternations in the fluorescence lifetime of donors [34]. As shown in Fig. 3F, The addition of Cu2+ resulted in a decrease in the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs from 14.5 ns to 12.3 ns, indicating that B-CDs underwent a dynamic quenching process through FRET between B-CDs and Y-CDs in the presence of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response behavior of GSH to Cu2+-BY-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The response behavior of GSH to Cu2+-BY-CDs was then explored due to the robust coordination between the –COOH and –NH2 groups of GSH with Cu2+, resulting in two GSH molecules chelating with each Cu2+ [35,36]. When GSH was introduced, the fluorescence intensity at 475 nm exhibited a gradual increase, while the fluorescence intensity of 580 nm decreased (Fig. 4 A). To achieve optimal detection performance, Initially, we conducted an investigation into the impact of Cu2+ concentration on the corresponding fluorescent ratio of F580/F475 in the presence of diverse GSH concentration. 10 μM Cu2+ was thus selected as the satisfied value due to its higher fluorescent ratio upon GSH addition (Figs. S10 and S11). The F580/F475 ratio showed a significant decrease as the reaction time increased after the introduction of GSH, eventually reaching a plateau at 12 min of incubation (Fig. S12). The influence of incubation temperature (5 °C–45 °C) on the ratio value was displayed in Fig. S13, suggesting that 25 °C was the appropriate temperature for sensor construction. With an increase in GSH concentration, the sensor exhibited a significant enhancement in fluorescence emission at 475 nm and a substantial reduction at 580 nm (Fig. 4B), leading to a gradual reduction in the ratio value of F580/F475. The range of 10–100 μM GSH exhibited a robust linear correlation, with a calculated detection limit of 6.82 μM (Fig. 4C).

The fluorescence response of this sensor to various types of interfering substances was then investigated. As depicted in Fig. 4D, incorporating other potential interfering substances did not result in significant deviations in the ratio value of F580/F475, except for Cystein (Cys). The moderate influence of Cys might be ascribed to its similar molecular structure with GSH, but it has a comparatively week capacity for metal ion binding due to steric hindrance effect [36,37]. During the actual sample detection processes, interference from Cys can be disregarded due to its trace amounts in fruits and vegetables as well as the dilution procedure during the detection of real food samples [38].

The above-mentioned ratiometric fluorescence readout relies on intricate optical systems for subsequent data acquisition, thereby significantly limiting its practical application in POCT. To address this issue, we next developed a portable all-in-one device (150 mm × 75 mm x 55 mm) comprising UV light source, 3D-printed covers, tube holder (Fig. 5 A). The UV LED emits light at an incidence angle of 45° onto the sample tube, accompanied by fluorescence signals captured by the smartphone's camera. To enhance the accuracy of data extraction from fluorescence images, we employed a software tool named “Color Picker” for capturing RGB values, which was obtained by separating each image to the red, green, and blue components. Furthermore, these color codes can be discretized into 256 integer values according to their respective levels of brightness. The Cu2+-BY-CDs demonstrated a noticeable color transition from yellow to blue within the GSH concentration range of 0–200 μM, as illustrated in Fig. 5B. The red fluorescence intensity and R value decreased while the blue fluorescence intensity and B value increased with increasing GSH concentration (Fig. 5C), thus resulting in a reverse change of dual-signal fluorescence. The relationship between GSH concentration and the ratio value of R/B was linear within the range of 10–100 μM (Fig. 5D), with a limit of detection estimated to be 4.8 μM according to 3 s/m rule (s representes the standard deviation of the blank, while m indicates the slope of the linear dynamic range). To further validate the superiority of Cu2+-BY-CDs in GSH detection, a comparative analysis was carried out using only Y-CDs as single-signal response mode. As shown in Fig. S14, solely yellow fluorescence was monitored when the GSH concentration reached a sufficiently high level due to the specific interaction of Y-CDs and Cu2+. Consequently, accurately determining the GSH concentration based on color changes became challenging. The quantitative analysis was also performed using only the R value; with the LOD calculated to be approximately 7.66 μM, which is nearly two times higher than that that of our Cu2+-BY-CDs system.

To verify the universality of this portable device, a user-friendly sensor based on paper was fabricated by depositing both CDs directly onto a paper strip for visual measurement of GSH. As shown in Fig. 5E, the test paper with BY-CDs exhibited blue luminescence when excited by UV light source, indicating that the deposition of CDs on the paper did not result in self-quenching and could potentially be used for smartphone-assisted all-in-one device applications. After adding 20 μM GSH to the Cu2+-BY-CDs system, a distinguishable change in color was observed as demonstrated in Fig. 5F. The color transition from yellow to blue on the paper strip became increasingly prominent with higher GSH concentrations (Fig. S15), indicating the versatility of this ratio fluorescent sensor for portable and visual detection of GSH.

Due to the superior sensitivity and selectivity of Cu2+-BY-CDs sensor towards GSH, we proceeded to assess its reliability and practicality through spike recovery experiments in foods such as grapes and tomatoes. The results obtained from the portable device are presented in Table S1, illustrating recovery rates ranging from 93.8 % to 106 %, accompanied by a relative standard deviation (RSD) below 3.6 %. The analysis results obtained using this method were consistent with the commercial kit detection in food samples, including tomatoes, cherry tomatoes, cucumbers and green grapes, with an RSD below 3.1 % (Table 1 ). These findings unequivocally demonstrate the feasibility and accuracy of the designed ratiometric sensor in detecting GSH in real samples.

In summary, we have constructed a ratiometric fluorescent sensor that utilizes dual CDs covalently attached to Cu2+, which exhibits a favorable response to GSH. The experimental results confirm that the sensing mechanism can be attributed not only to Cu2+-induced energy transfer between B-CDs and Y-CDs but also to the specific coordination between Cu2+ and GSH. The fluorescence intensity increases at 475 nm while simultaneously decreasing at 580 nm, resulting in a noticeable color shift from yellow to blue. With two reversible signal variations for ratiometric design, this Cu2+-BY-CDs system can be integrated into an affordable and portable 3D-printed device without an external light source, enabling accurate detection of GSH in food samples using RGB analysis. Furthermore, the universality of this portable device was validated on a paper strip for GSH sensing. This all-in-one system offers practical applications in food safety and nutrition, which have significant implications for public health.

Yuan Gao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Bingfeng Bai: Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Zenghui Mao: Resources, Project administration, Investigation. Yi Shi: Methodology, Investigation. Bo Zhang: Validation, Investigation. Ke Quan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Zhihe Qing: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (32001782, 22474012，22222402, 32302222), and the open research fund of Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Regional Hereditary Birth Defects Prevention and Control (HPKL2023025), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2024JJ3001) and the Aid Program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Higher Educational Institutions of Hunan Province (2023ct01）.
